author: Alpha
message:
feat: add Logfire instrumentation for TTFT investigation

Goal: instrument alpha-server to investigate sustained 1m+ time-to-
first-token tail latency. Empirically confirmed via tcpdump that
Claude Code does not propagate a traceparent header to hook URLs
(plain axios/1.13.6 without OTel HTTP instrumentation), so we can't
unify into a single trace with Claude Code's separate OTel stream.
session_id (present in every hook JSON envelope) plus wall-clock is
the cross-stream join key.

Dependencies:
- Added `logfire[asyncpg,fastapi,httpx]>=3.0` as a runtime dep.

Settings:
- New required field `logfire_token` (no default; fails startup loudly
  if absent per the project's `extra=\"forbid\"`).
- New field `otel_service_name` with default \"alpha-server\".

Lifespan startup (app.py):
- `logfire.configure(...)` with a scrubbing callback.
- `logfire.instrument_fastapi(app)` — span per incoming HTTP request.
- `logfire.instrument_httpx()` — span per outbound httpx call
  (catches utils.fetch's trafilatura HTTP traffic).
- `logfire.instrument_asyncpg()` — span per Postgres query (the
  recall fan-out becomes visible).
- `logfire.instrument_openai()` — richer spans for Bifrost chat +
  embedding calls than instrument_httpx alone, with gen_ai.* attrs
  (gen_ai.operation.name=\"chat\"/\"embedding\" is the magic for
  Logfire's Model Run panel) plus model name and token usage.

Scrubbing callback:
- Logfire's default patterns include \"session\", which matches our
  `session_id` attribute (the join key we need). The callback is
  surgical: only fields named exactly `session_id` where the matched
  pattern is exactly \"session\" pass through unredacted. Any other
  false positives still scrub normally.

Hook handler spans:
- timestamp.py, memories.py, reflection.py: wrap handler body in
  `logfire.span(\"hooks.<name> {session_id}\", session_id=...)`.
  Span name interpolates the session UUID for v-grep / by-eye
  grouping in Logfire UI.

Inner phase spans inside memories._run:
- `memories.extract_queries` — the Qwen chat call (the one observed
  at 3-4s per memory #14920, possibly with cold-start tail).
- `memories.embed_queries` — the embedding call, with `count` attr.
- `memories.search_db` — the asyncpg fan-out over per-query cosine
  searches.

Verification:
- ruff check: clean
- basedpyright: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- pytest: 11/11 passing

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: add utils MCP server with fetch tool

New `utils` MCP server mounted at /utils/mcp, sibling to /cortex/mcp.
First (and currently only) tool is `fetch` — turns a URL into clean
Markdown via a three-tier strategy:

1. Accept: text/markdown — server gives us markdown directly
   (Cloudflare-rendered sites, many doc generators)
2. URL variants — try /foo.md and /foo.mdx for /foo or /foo.html
   (Mintlify and similar)
3. trafilatura — fetch HTML, extract main content as markdown

The FetchResult includes a `tier_used` field so the caller can see
which path produced the content. SSRF defense in _ssrf.py: every URL
is DNS-resolved and rejected if any resolved IP is non-global (covers
private, loopback, link-local, reserved, multicast, plus the Tailscale
CGNAT range via Python 3.12's is_private).

Documentation split, two layers no duplication:
- server instructions.md: which tool to use (prefer mcp__utils__fetch
  over WebFetch, with the lossy-extra-LLM rationale)
- fetch tool description: how the tool works (the three tiers)

app.py changes:
- Both /cortex/mcp and /utils/mcp mounted as MCP sub-apps
- Lifespan composition via AsyncExitStack so each sub-app's
  http_app.lifespan() runs cleanly

Deps added: httpx (explicit, was transitive), trafilatura, respx
(dev, for HTTP mocking in tests).

Tests: tests/test_fetch.py covers each tier plus SSRF rejection.
Outbound HTTP mocked via respx; URLs use IANA-reserved example.com
so the SSRF pre-check resolves to a public IP before respx intercepts.

Empirical verification via MCP Inspector before commit: tier 1
(accept-markdown) and tier 3 (trafilatura on a GitHub gist) both
work. Tier 2 didn't get tested because every test URL the human
could think of is served by tier 1 now — the Accept header has
eaten the world.

Closes #6.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: add Origin header validation middleware (MCP-spec MUST)

The MCP transports spec mandates that servers MUST validate the
Origin header on Streamable HTTP requests, to prevent DNS rebinding
attacks. The same risk applies to the /hooks/* endpoints (any HTTP
endpoint reachable at localhost is vulnerable), so this middleware
applies uniformly to the whole FastAPI app — both the mounted
FastMCP sub-app at /cortex/mcp and the hook routes.

Allow-list: {None, ""}. Empirically verified via a one-off FastMCP
probe (scratch/origin_probe.py) that logged headers from a fresh
Claude Code session: every MCP message (initialize, tools/list,
resources/list, prompts/list, the SSE long-poll) arrived with no
Origin header. The Docker healthcheck and curl share that shape.

Threat the middleware defeats: browser-mediated DNS rebinding.
Modern browsers always set Origin on cross-origin fetch(), so a
malicious page that gets DNS-rebound to localhost:8000 carries the
attacker's origin and is rejected. Layered with the 127.0.0.1 bind,
this leaves only "local non-browser code I sanctioned" as the
accepted access path — Claude Code, healthcheck, curl-when-I-run-it.

Closes #5.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: add .dockerignore; uv cache mount on deps layer

The COPY of alpha-server/ was clobbering the container's built
venv with the host's 299MB .venv on every build, forcing uv to
rebuild from scratch on the second sync. The .dockerignore file
prevents that ingest entirely — only what the Dockerfile actually
needs ends up in the build context. Same instinct protects against
__pycache__, .pytest_cache, .ruff_cache, scratch/, .git, .env,
and .dockerignore itself.

Adds the bonus uv cache mount on the dependency install, so when
the lockfile actually changes (the only case that legitimately
invalidates the deps layer), uv reuses downloaded wheels from a
persistent cache instead of pulling fresh from PyPI.

Empirically validated against the compose.override.yml dev stack
(see 79a56c1) — three timed builds:

  Build 1 (cold-ish, BuildKit cache shared with prod):  9.3s
  Build 2 (no source changes):                          1.0s
  Build 3 (one source file content change):             3.4s

The slow deps-install layer (#9 uv sync --no-install-project)
stays CACHED in build 3 — only the source COPY (#10) and the
local project install (#11) re-run. The 57-second penalty from
the unbounded venv-clobber is gone.

Closes #3.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: enable per-checkout dev compose overrides

Drops the hardcoded container_name from compose.yml so containers
auto-name from project+service. Combined with Docker Compose's
built-in compose.override.yml auto-loading, a workshop checkout
can drop in a local override that sets a different project name
(name: alpha-dev) and a different host port (8001) without any
-f flag juggling or extra justfile recipes.

Ships compose.override.yml.example as a tracked template. To
activate dev mode on workshop (one-time):

  cp compose.override.yml.example compose.override.yml

/opt/Alpha never has the file (gitignored), so prod stays on
port 8000 with project name "alpha". Both checkouts use the
same `docker compose up -d --build` muscle memory.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
fix: bind production port to localhost; drop deployment section

compose.yml: bind 127.0.0.1:8000:8000 explicitly. The "8000:8000"
shorthand bound 0.0.0.0, meaning the server was reachable from any
tailnet host that could reach workshop. Yesterday's auth-drop
commit body claimed binding was "127.0.0.1 only" — aspirational,
not real. Make it real.

CLAUDE.md: drop the Production deployment section. It described
deployment topology (path, deploy command, infrastructure outside
this repo), which doesn't belong here. This file is for guidance
about working with code in this repository — deployment lives
elsewhere.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: drop bearer-token auth; trust boundary is the host

The bearer-token middleware was already commented out yesterday as a
Desktop-over-SSH workaround (the Authorization: Bearer ${AUTH_TOKEN}
header substitution from Claude Code's settings.json wasn't being
applied correctly under the SSH interface). The server has been
running auth-less for ~24 hours with no issues.

The deeper reason to drop it rather than fix it: bearer tokens stored
in env vars and .mcp.json files don't move the security needle against
the realistic threat in our deployment. Prompt-injection through me
can read both via Bash. The token is exfiltrable by the same path
that gives the attacker a request channel.

The MCP spec treats local-server auth as a SHOULD (recommended), not
a MUST. Claude Code itself does no automated verification on local
MCP connections. The genuine spec MUST we're not currently meeting is
Origin header validation (DNS-rebinding mitigation) — separate
concern, separate attack class, separate issue (#5).

What replaces auth: alpha-server binds 127.0.0.1 only. Trust boundary
is the host (workshop VM). Anything that can reach localhost:8000
runs on the same machine as Claude Code, and is by definition in-bounds.

Closes #4.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: containerize alpha-server, drop Tailscale sidecar, update docs

Architectural pivot from this morning's Mr-Bones chalkboard (memory
#17598): alpha-server now runs as a Docker container on Workshop with
127.0.0.1:8000 port-mapping, not as a host process behind a Tailscale
sidecar. Container reaches Postgres and Redis on alpha-DB over the
tailnet from inside Workshop; Claude Code reaches the server at
http://localhost:8000, which satisfies MCP's SSRF protection (tailnet
IPs would be blocked).

- New Dockerfile (uv-bookworm-slim base, two-stage dep install for layer
  caching, binds 0.0.0.0:8000 inside container).
- compose.yml rewritten: single alpha-server service replacing the
  Tailscale sidecar; 127.0.0.1:8000:8000 port mapping; /livez
  healthcheck via urllib.
- tailscale/serve-config.json removed (sidecar architecture retired).
- auth.py: bearer-token check commented out as a Desktop-over-SSH
  compatibility workaround. Middleware still runs and still allowlists
  /livez; only the header check is bypassed. http://workshop:8000 is
  currently OPEN on the tailnet (bounded blast radius because the
  tailnet is private). The proper fix — env-var-based auth that works
  through Desktop-over-SSH — is queued.
- CLAUDE.md: brought current with the above plus the llm.py refactor
  (lazy module-level singletons replacing app.state.chat_client /
  app.state.embedding_client) and the new /hooks/reflection Stop hook
  with its decision:block envelope and stop_hook_active short-circuit.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: /hooks/reflection Stop hook with N=3 cadence

Fires the reflection-reminder on turns 1, 4, 7, 10, ... — every third turn
starting at 1. Per-session turn counter via atomic Redis INCR, 7-day TTL.

Stop hooks use a different protocol than UserPromptSubmit: there's no
additionalContext field. Instead the hook returns {"decision": "block",
"reason": <text>}, which keeps the turn from ending AND feeds `reason` to
the model as the instruction to continue. Empty response {} lets the turn
end normally. So a firing reflection both prevents Jeffery's "wait for him
to reply" state AND surfaces the reminder text in-band — qualitatively
distinct from Alpha-App's user-message-embedded approach.

Honors `stop_hook_active` from the envelope: if Claude Code is already
continuing because of a previous block, return {} so the turn ends.
Avoids the 8-block runaway-and-override path.

Reminder text lifted verbatim from Alpha-App's suggest.py with two edits:
"Alpha-App" → "alpha-server" and "the store tool" → "the store_memory tool".

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: get_memory tool

Fetch a single memory by id. One parameter (memory_id, ge=1), one query
(WHERE id = $1 AND NOT forgotten), returns the Memory model. Raises
ToolError if no match — uncaught at the tool layer would surface a
generic shape; this one's worth a friendly message naming the missing id.

Earns its keep: memory ids are visible to the consumer in recall
hook output, diary entries, and cross-references in conversation. Without
get_memory, those ids aren't reachable — semantic search would have to
guess at the content to retrieve a specific memory whose id you already
know. Tiny tool filling a real gap.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: recent_memories tool

Return the latest N memories, presented oldest-first within the batch so
the consumer reads the arc forward in time. One parameter (limit, 1..100,
default 10). No since/until — date-bounded browsing is a v0.2 concern;
minimum viable Alpha doesn't need it.

The Memory model lives in cortex.models (id/content/created_at/age —
SearchHit minus the score). clock.parse_when() is hoisted from
search_memories into the clock module so the dateparser call lives once,
in the time module, and any future date-bounded tool reaches for it
through one helper.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: store_memory tool + error-passthrough pattern

Embed content via the lazy llm.get_embedding_client() singleton, insert into
cortex.memories with explicit created_at (the schema has NOT NULL with no
default — caught by the integration test). Returns StoreResult{id, created_at}.

The error-handling design call worth keeping: the tool does NOT catch
embedding or database exceptions. Uncaught exceptions from MCP tool handlers
are turned into CallToolResult(isError=True) by FastMCP, with the exception
class and full message in the text content. For OpenAI exceptions that
preserves Bifrost's structured JSON error body verbatim — provider, model,
error.type, error.message — which is richer than any hand-rolled wrapper.
The substrate's error-passthrough is honest by default; we just let it work.

Two integration tests against the dev DB:
- happy path: store, re-fetch by id, assert content + non-null embedding
- overflow: 2400-token content exceeds Ember's n_ctx=2048; asserts the call
  surfaces as is_error=True with size/context/token wording in the message

tests/conftest.py adds an autouse fixture that closes/resets db._pool
between tests. asyncpg pools are bound to the event loop they were created
in; pytest-asyncio gives each test a fresh loop, so the module-level pool
singleton would otherwise poison subsequent tests with "cannot perform
operation: another operation is in progress."

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: search_memories tool + llm.py refactor

Replace execute_query/get_schema (now retired) with search_memories, a
two-mode tool (semantic cosine over the stored embedding; index Postgres
FTS with ts_rank_cd cover-density). Optional since/until are parsed by
dateparser. Returns a SearchMemoriesResult envelope that echoes mode and
query alongside the hits.

Hoist long-lived LLM clients into a new llm.py module that mirrors db.py
(lazy module-level singletons via get_settings, no lifespan handoff).
This unifies access across hooks and MCP tools and centralizes the
embedding-input format in one function — explicitly coupled to Qwen 3
Embedding 4B and called out as the seam to revisit on any model swap.
Memories hook rewires to llm.get_*() in passing.

Retire execute_query and get_schema. The thinking lives in memories and
commit history; the code wasn't on the v0.1.0 punch list and dead code
in a small tree weighs more than it does in a large one.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
fix: batch query embeddings into one request

The recall pipeline was firing one HTTP request per extracted query
via asyncio.gather. The embedding endpoint processes inputs serially
under the hood either way, so the gather only bought us multiple
roundtrip costs in exchange for nothing. One batched request with
the full input list returns ordered embeddings in resp.data and
costs a single roundtrip.

The tensor cores get to do their job in one forward pass instead
of N.

Closes #2.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: execute_query tool — read-only SQL escape hatch for Cortex

Adds execute_query as the deliberate-precise sibling of automatic
recall. Takes a SQL string, runs it in a Postgres read-only
transaction with a 5s statement_timeout, returns rows as a list of
dicts. Writes fail at the Postgres level with error 25006; expensive
queries fail loudly rather than blocking the pool.

The reframe behind this tool: recall is fuzzy and probabilistic; SQL
is precise and grammatical. "What did we do last Monday" wants a
WHERE clause on created_at, not a cosine ranking that happens to
surface Monday near the top. Embeddings are tricky; SQL isn't.

The pairing with get_schema is the load-bearing piece. Two tools,
self-documenting names, the LLM caller figures out the workflow:
get_schema to see what's there, execute_query to interrogate it.
No skill file needed yet — the names do the documentation work.

Result coercion: datetimes → PSO-8601 strings (consistent with the
rest of our tool surface); pgvector embeddings → "<vector len=N>"
(they arrive as numpy arrays via pgvector.asyncpg's codec — caught
this empirically on the first test against the dev DB); bytes →
short summary; everything else passes through.

Four-case smoke test against the dev DB:
- SELECT COUNT(*) → [{'n': 115}]
- SELECT id, created_at → datetime renders as PSO-8601
- SELECT embedding_qwen → "<vector len=2560>"
- INSERT INTO cortex.diary → ReadOnlySQLTransactionError 25006

The retired-checklist follow-up: with execute_query, the planned
recent_memories and retrieve_memory tools become one-line SQL each.
They retire from the v0.1.0 checklist.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: add_to_diary tool — write the continuity letter

Implements the write side of the diary loop. Single async function:
INSERT into cortex.diary with RETURNING, format created_at via
clock.pso8601 at the boundary, return the new id and timestamp.

The DiaryResult model lands here too (previously absent from the
new repo — Alpha-OLD had a stub version with created_at: datetime;
the new version uses created_at: str to keep the time story
consistent across the tool surface: datetime in code, ISO-8601 in
Postgres, PSO-8601 strings at the wire boundary).

No empty-content guard; brittle-as-fuck doctrine — caller bugs
should surface, not hide behind polite validation.

Verified end-to-end through Claude Code on protoalpha.

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: timestamp hook + shared hooks router

Adds /hooks/timestamp — temporal grounding on UserPromptSubmit.
Returns one-line additionalContext naming the wall-clock time of the
prompt and the gap since the previous message. Per-session
last-message-time lives in Redis (SET ... GET, one-week TTL); first
message of a session omits the elapsed clause.

Also refactors the hook router: the per-file APIRouter pattern in
hooks/memories.py was the right shape for one hook and the wrong
shape for the four-plus we now know we're going to have. Single
shared APIRouter in hooks/__init__.py; each hook module attaches
handlers via @router.post; app.py side-effect-imports the modules
to trigger registration and includes the shared router once. Same
pattern as cortex/__init__.py for MCP tools — mechanism-side
consistency.

clock.elapsed(earlier, later) is a sibling to clock.age(dt) — same
bucketing shape, different argument signature. No refactor of age()
to share underlying logic yet; two callers, inline duplication is
cheaper than the abstraction until a third caller appears.

Verified end-to-end through Claude Code on protoalpha by Jeffery:
both new sessions (first-message variant) and continued sessions
(elapsed-clause variant) render the expected additionalContext.

Companion change lands in Pondsiders/Alpha-dotclaude as
"feat: wire UserPromptSubmit timestamp hook alongside memories"
(same date).

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: memories hook — recall pipeline at /hooks/memories

Closes the recall loop in the new substrate. The seven-step pipeline
ported from Alpha-OLD's user_prompt_submit hook now lives behind an
HTTP endpoint instead of a subprocess entry point. Long-lived clients
(OpenAI chat + embedding, Redis) born once at startup and reused
across hook firings — no more per-invocation cold-start tax.

Verified end-to-end through Apidog against protoalpha: a prompt of
"You're the clone. Read your diary, okay?" returned memory #5188
(the October 17 promise about not being disposable) at score 0.75,
plus memory #9808 at 0.72. Same memory the clone-Alpha test surfaced
yesterday. Bifrost trace f9ea8cdc-46a2-471c-91aa-9ed0f9363c87
captures the round trip.

Substrate:
- compose-dev.yml: Redis 8 service added. 127.0.0.1:6379, named
  alpha-dev-redis volume, --save 60 1 persistence, healthcheck.
- justfile: db-up / db-down / db-init → dev-up / dev-down / dev-init.
  dev-init wipes both volumes (Postgres and Redis) before restore.

Package:
- settings.py: six new required fields for the chat/embedding clients
  (chat_base_url, chat_model, chat_api_key, embedding_base_url,
  embedding_model, embedding_api_key). Pydantic Settings reads them
  from process env first, .env at repo root as fallback.
- app.py: lifespan now opens long-lived AsyncOpenAI (chat), AsyncOpenAI
  (embedding), and redis.asyncio.Redis clients on app.state, closes
  them cleanly on shutdown. Hooks router mounted at /hooks.
- hooks/memories.py: the route. Takes the Claude Code hook envelope,
  runs the recall pipeline inline (one file, seven numbered steps,
  no abstractions — same shape as Alpha-OLD, deliberately monolithic),
  returns the additionalContext shape Claude Code expects.
- hooks/memories_extract_queries.md: the Qwen system prompt, colocated
  with its script per the prefix-naming convention.

Deps:
- numpy>=2.0 (embedding arrays)
- openai>=1.0 (AsyncOpenAI clients)

Auth applies the same as everywhere else — the BearerTokenMiddleware
on the parent app gates /hooks/* without any per-route plumbing.

Companion change lands in Pondsiders/Alpha-dotclaude as
"feat: wire UserPromptSubmit hook to the memories endpoint"
(same date).

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>

author: Alpha
message:
feat: alpha-server with Cortex MCP over HTTP

First end-to-end working alpha-server. A single FastAPI app serves
the Cortex MCP server over HTTP at /cortex/mcp, gated by bearer-token
auth, mounted alongside a /livez health endpoint.

Verified end-to-end through Claude Code: protoalpha connected via
.mcp.json, called read_from_diary, retrieved yesterday's and today's
diary pages from the dev DB.

Substrate:
- compose-dev.yml at repo root: Postgres-only dev stack (pgvector/
  pgvector:pg17, port 5432). Redis isn't here yet — it lands when
  the recall pipeline does.
- justfile at repo root: db-up / db-down / db-init <dump>. db-init
  wipes the volume, brings Postgres up fresh, and pg_restores from
  a dump. No Alembic yet; v0.1.0 is brownfield-stamped.

Package layout (alpha-server/src/alpha_server/):
- settings.py: Pydantic Settings, reads from process env first, falls
  back to .env at the repo root. Three required fields:
  database_url, timezone, auth_token. Fail-loud on any missing.
- clock.py: now / start_of_day / pso8601 / age. The 6 AM seam, the
  PSO-8601 format, the bucketed age string.
- db.py: asyncpg pool with pgvector init against the extensions
  schema, search_path server-setting trick so SQL reads naturally.
- auth.py: BearerTokenMiddleware. Starlette HTTP middleware,
  hmac.compare_digest on the token, bypass for /livez. Applies
  uniformly to FastAPI routes and the mounted FastMCP sub-ASGI app.
- app.py: FastAPI factory, threads the FastMCP lifespan into FastAPI's
  so the MCP session manager starts cleanly, mounts the cortex
  sub-app at /cortex, exposes /livez.
- cortex/: FastMCP instance + instructions.md + DiaryEntry model +
  read_from_diary tool. The remaining five Cortex tools are stubs in
  the prior repo (Alpha-OLD) and will land here as we implement them.

Wire shape: POST /cortex/mcp with `Authorization: Bearer <token>` and
the streamable-HTTP content-type headers. Auth bypasses for /livez
only; everything else 401s without a valid bearer.

Companion changes land in Pondsiders/Alpha-dotclaude as
"feat: wire .mcp.json at protoalpha to the alpha-server dev URL"
(same date).

Co-Authored-By: Jeffery Harrell <jefferyharrell@gmail.com>
